text: The surgeon begins the laparoscopic cholecystectomy by entering the abdomen through an open technique at the umbilicus. Peritoneal incisions are made anteriorly and extended cranially, revealing the underlying structures.
The surgeon dissects the fibrofatty tissue to delineate Calot's triangle, incising the edge of the peritoneal incision to enhance exposure.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection over the fibrofatty tissue using a monopolar hook electrode with coagulating current, carefully separating fibers to maintain meticulous hemostasis.

The surgeon observes the transparency of the peritoneal reflection and makes an incision over the posterior peritoneum, extending it superiorly. After completing the posterior incision, the surgeon returns to the anterior side, creating a peritoneal window.

The surgeon continues dissection within the Calot's triangle, focusing on removing fibrofatty tissue to achieve the critical view of safety. The cystic artery is being carefully delineated as part of the procedure.

The cystic artery and duct are visible as the surgeon performs gradual fiber-by-fiber dissection. The Maryland Dissector is used to skeletonize the cystic duct, stripping the peritoneum and dissecting fatty tissue to prepare for clip application.

The surgeon has nearly achieved the critical view of safety, with the Calot's triangle cleared of fat and fibrous tissue. Two structures are visible entering the gallbladder, and the lower one-third of the cystic plate is exposed.
The surgeon uses a smooth grasper in the left hand to dissect the final attachments to the cystic duct, removing the last few fibers surrounding it.
The surgeon uses an advanced bipolar device to coagulate the cystic artery, ensuring hemostasis before employing the bipolar cutting function to sever the coagulated artery.

The surgeon milks the cystic duct to ensure it is free of stones, then applies a Lager clip to secure the duct. Two clips are placed on the cystic duct side and one clip is positioned superiorly on the specimen side.
The surgeon applies a clip to the specimen side of the cystic duct, ensuring a gap between the clips. A curved scissor is then used to cut the clipped duct, completing the separation.
The surgeon uses a bipolar energy device to cut fibrous tissue in the lumen, addressing potential bleeding by employing the bipolar cutting function.

The surgeon switches to a hook instrument and applies traction with the left hand while using coagulation current. Alternating energy with blunt pushing, the surgeon makes lateral peritoneal incisions to dissect the gallbladder from the liver bed, taking care to avoid tearing the gallbladder.

The surgeon applies proper traction to assist in dissecting the gallbladder from the liver bed, using a monopolar hook to address dense fibrous attachments. The left hand instrument is repositioned closer to the dissecting area near the infundibulum for better control.
The surgeon inspects the cystic duct stem, confirming there is no bleeding. The final attachments of the gallbladder are dissected off the liver bed, ensuring complete hemostasis.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the gallbladder by addressing the last fibrous attachments and ensuring hemostasis. The gallbladder is then removed intact through the epigastric port after suctioning and removing bile.
